1: Psalm 44 is the first of the communal laments — the "we" of the nation rather than the "I" of the individual. אֱלֹהִים בְּאָזְנֵינוּ שָׁמַעְנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm bĕʾāznênû shāmaʿnû, "O God, we have heard with our own ears") — bĕʾāznênû: "with our ears" — the auditory reception of tradition. אֲבוֹתֵינוּ סִפְּרוּ-לָנוּ (ʾăbôtênû sippĕrû-lānû, "our ancestors have told us what you accomplished") — sāpar (piel: to recount, to narrate — the word behind mizmôr and the ongoing declaration of Ps 19:1). The historical tradition as oral narrative passed from generation to generation.
2: אַתָּה יָדְךָ גּוֹיִם הוֹרַשְׁתָּ (ʾattāh yādĕkā gôyim hôrashtā, "With your own hand you drove out the nations") — yārash (hiphil: to dispossess, to drive out): the Conquest vocabulary. וַתִּטָּעֵם (wattiṭṭāʿêm, "and planted our ancestors") — nāṭaʿ: to plant (as trees are planted) — the ancestors as a planted community in their land. תָּרַע לְאֻמִּים וַתְּשַׁלְּחֵם (tārǎʿ lĕʾummîm wattĕshallĕḥêm, "you crushed the peoples and expelled them") — the two-stage action: crush (rāʿaʿ: to shatter, to break in pieces) then expel (shālaḥ: to send away, to release).
3: כִּי לֹא בְחַרְבָּם יָרְשׁוּ אָרֶץ (kî lōʾ bĕḥarbām yārĕshû ʾāreṣ, "For they did not win the land by their own sword") — the theological interpretation of the Conquest: not by their own military prowess. וּזְרוֹעָם לֹא-הוֹשִׁיעָה לָּמוֹ (ûzĕrôʿām lōʾ-hôshîʿāh llāmô, "and their own arm did not deliver them") — zĕrôaʿ: arm (the symbol of human strength and military power). כִּי-יְמִינְךָ וּזְרוֹעֲךָ (kî-yĕmînĕkā ûzĕrôʿĕkā, "it was your right hand and your arm") — the same word zĕrôaʿ (arm) now applied to God: the divine arm accomplished what the human arm could not. וְאוֹר פָּנֶיךָ כִּי רְצִיתָם (wĕʾôr pānêkā kî rĕṣîtām, "and the light of your face, because you were favorable toward them") — rāṣāh: to be favorable, to take pleasure in — the divine pleasure as the ultimate reason for the conquest.
4: אַתָּה-הוּא מַלְכִּי אֱלֹהִים (ʾattāh-hûʾ malkî ʾĕlōhîm, "You are my King, O God") — the shift from the communal recitation to the first-person singular: the individual or royal voice within the communal psalm. צַוֵּה יְשׁוּעוֹת יַעֲקֹב (ṣawwêh yĕshûʿôt yaʿăqōb, "decree victories for Jacob") — ṣāwāh (piel: to command, to decree): the divine word of command that actualizes salvation. Yĕshûʿôt (plural: salvations — multiple acts of rescue).
5: בְּךָ צָרֵינוּ נְנַגֵּחַ (bĕkā ṣārênû nĕnaggêaḥ, "Through you we push back our enemies") — nāgaḥ: to gore, to push (as a bull uses its horns). בְּשִׁמְךָ נָבוּס קָמֵינוּ (bĕshimkā nābûs qāmênû, "in your name we trample those who rise against us") — bûs: to trample with the feet. Both verbs are animal-strength images — the horn-push and the hoof-trample — applied to the community's combat, but "through you" and "in your name" locate the power in the divine name, not in the community's own strength.
6: כִּי לֹא בְקַשְׁתִּי אֶבְטָח (kî lōʾ bĕqashtî ʾebṭāḥ, "For I do not trust in my bow") — qeshet: a bow — the primary long-range weapon. Bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on (the recurring trust-verb). The explicit renunciation of military self-reliance — the bow as the symbol of personal martial capacity.
7: כִּי הוֹשַׁעְתָּנוּ מִצָּרֵינוּ (kî hôshaʿtānû miṣṣārênû, "But you have saved us from our enemies") — yāshaʿ (hiphil: to cause to be saved, to rescue): God as the active saver — the hiphil causative shows God bringing salvation into being, not merely permitting it. וּמְשַׂנְאֵינוּ הֱבִישׁוֹתָ (ûmĕśanĕʾênû hĕbîshôtā, "and disgraced those who hate us") — bûsh (hiphil: to cause to be ashamed): God shaming the enemies — the reversal of the taunt.
8: בֵּאלֹהִים הִלַּלְנוּ כָל-הַיּוֹם (bêʾlōhîm hillalnû kol-hayyôm, "In God we have boasted all day long") — hālal (piel: to praise, to boast about — the same root as hallĕlûyāh). The community's continuous boasting is entirely "in God" (bêʾlōhîm) — not in their own history or identity. Selāh marks the transition to the lament that follows.
9: אַף-זָנַחְתָּ וַתַּכְלִימֵנוּ (ʾap-zānaḥtā wattaklîmênû, "But you have cast us off and brought us to disgrace") — the shocking reversal: the same God who saved (v7) has now rejected (zānaḥ: to cast away — the covenant-rejection word). Kālam (hiphil: to bring to shame, to disgrace): public humiliation. וְלֹא-תֵצֵא בְּצִבְאֹתֵינוּ (wĕlōʾ-têṣêʾ bĕṣibʾôtênû, "and have not gone out with our armies") — the divine warrior who fought with Israel (v3) is no longer accompanying the armies. The divine desertion of the military host.
10: תְּשִׁיבֵנוּ אָחוֹר מִנִּי-צָר (tĕshîbênû ʾāḥôr minnî-ṣār, "You made us retreat before the enemy") — shûb (hiphil: to cause to turn back): not voluntary retreat but forced turning. Ṣār: the adversary (the same ṣar root as distress — the enemy as the tightening force). וּמְשַׂנְאֵינוּ שָׁסוּ לָמוֹ (ûmĕśanĕʾênû shāsû lāmô, "and our enemies plundered us") — shāsāh: to plunder, to despoil — taking what belongs to the defeated.
11: תִּתְּנֵנוּ כְּצֹאן מַאֲכָל (tittĕnênû kĕṣōʾn maʾăkāl, "You handed us over like sheep for slaughter") — ṣōʾn: the flock; maʾăkāl: food — the sheep as food for those who devour them. The passive submission of the defenseless flock. This verse anticipates Ps 44:22, which Paul quotes directly. וּבַגּוֹיִם זֵרִיתָנוּ (ûbaggôyim zêrîtānû, "and scattered us among the nations") — zārāh: to scatter, to winnow (as grain is scattered to the winds): the diaspora-scattering as divine action.
12: תִּמְכֹּר-עַמְּךָ בְלֹא-הוֹן (timkōr-ʿammĕkā bĕlōʾ-hôn, "You sold your people for a pittance") — mākar: to sell — the commercial vocabulary applied shockingly to God's people. Hôn: wealth, something of value. "Without wealth" — sold for nothing, the sale making no profit. The degradation is total: not even a fair price for the defeat.
13: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ חֶרְפָּה לִשְׁכֵנֵינוּ (tĕśîmênû ḥerpāh lishkênênû, "You have made us the object of mockery to our neighbors") — ḥerpāh: reproach, scorn (the same word as Ps 31:11 — the individual's shame now the community's). לַעַג וָקֶלֶס לִסְבִיבוֹתֵינוּ (laʿag wāqeles lisĕbîbôtênû, "a laughingstock and a source of scorn to those around us") — laʿag: mockery, derision; qeles: scorn, ridicule (only here and Ezek 36:4 — rare word). The community encircled by the laughter of their neighbors.
14: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ מָשָׁל בַּגּוֹיִם (tĕśîmênû māshāl baggôyim, "You have made us a byword among the nations") — māshāl: a proverb, a byword (to "become a māshāl" means to be a cautionary tale — Deut 28:37 threatens this as a covenant curse). מְנוֹד-רֹאשׁ בַּלְאֻמִּים (mĕnôd-rōʾsh ballĕʾummîm, "a wagging of heads among the peoples") — the body-language of contempt: the head-wagging that signals disdain or pitying dismissal (Jer 18:16; Lam 2:15).
15: כָּל-הַיּוֹם כְּלִמָּתִי נֶגְדִּי (kol-hayyôm kĕlimmātî negdî, "All day long my disgrace is before me") — kĕlimmāh: disgrace, shame — related to kālam (to be disgraced, v9). וּבֹשֶׁת פָּנַי כִּסָּתְנִי (ûbōshet pānay kissātĕnî, "and shame has covered my face") — bōshet: shame; kāsāh: to cover (the same verb as the "covered sin" of Ps 32:1, but now shame rather than sin is what covers). The face as the site of honor/shame — covered with shame rather than shining with the divine face-light.
16: מִקּוֹל מְחָרֵף וּמְגַדֵּף (miqqôl mĕḥārêp ûmĕgaddêp, "On account of the taunting voice of the one who reviles and blasphemes") — ḥārap (piel: to taunt, to revile); gādap (piel: to blaspheme, to revile — particularly of speaking against God). The voices of the enemies who simultaneously mock the people and blaspheme God. מִפְּנֵי אוֹיֵב וּמִתְנַקֵּם (mippĕnê ʾôyêb ûmitnakkêm, "because of the enemy who seeks revenge") — nāqam (hithpael: to avenge oneself, to seek revenge): the vindictive purpose behind the attack.
17: כָּל-זֹאת בָּאַתְנוּ וְלֹא שְׁכַחֲנוּךָ (kol-zōʾt bāʾatnû wĕlōʾ shĕkaḥănûkā, "All this has come upon us, yet we have not forgotten you") — the central theological claim of the psalm: the suffering is not the result of covenant infidelity (contrast Deut 28 where disaster follows apostasy). Shākaḥ: to forget — the covenant violation of forgetting God. The community denies this. וְלֹא-שִׁקַּרְנוּ בִּבְרִיתֶךָ (wĕlōʾ-shiqarnû bibrîtekā, "and we have not been unfaithful to your covenant") — shāqar: to be false, to deal falsely, to lie — the covenant (the bĕrît) not violated. This is the psalm of innocent suffering — the lament that cannot explain its pain by appeal to guilt.
18: לֹא-נָסוֹג אָחוֹר לִבֵּנוּ (lōʾ-nāsôg ʾāḥôr libbênû, "Our heart has not turned away") — nāsag: to turn back, to retreat (the same verb as "retreat" in v10, now applied to the heart rather than the army — the inward loyalty contrasted with the external military defeat). וַתֵּט אֲשֻׁרֵינוּ מִנִּי-אָרְחֶךָ (wattêṭ ʾăshurênû minnî-ʾārĕḥekā, "and our steps have not strayed from your path") — nāṭāh: to deviate, to turn aside. The path (ʾōraḥ) as the way of covenantal life — not abandoned.
19: כִּי דִכִּיתָנוּ בִּמְקוֹם תַּנִּים (kî dikkîtānû bimqôm tannîm, "Yet you have crushed us in the haunt of jackals") — dākāh (piel: to crush, to shatter — as the bones are crushed). Tannîm: jackals or sea-monsters (the same word appears as both — here contextually the jackals of the wilderness, the animals that inhabit desolate, destroyed places). וַתְּכַס עָלֵינוּ בְּצַלְמָוֶת (wattĕkas ʿālênû bĕṣalmāwet, "and covered us with the shadow of death") — ṣalmāwet: deep darkness, shadow of death (Ps 23:4 — the valley of ṣalmāwet). The total encirclement by lethal darkness.
20: אִם-שָׁכַחְנוּ שֵׁם אֱלֹהֵינוּ (ʾim-shākaḥnû shêm ʾĕlōhênû, "If we had forgotten the name of our God") — the conditional self-examination: "if we had done X..." — the ʾim (if) is an oath-structure in Hebrew: "if we had forgotten… [then let the punishment stand, but we haven't]." וַנִּפְרֹשׂ כַּפֵּינוּ לְאֵל זָר (wanniprōś kappênû lĕʾêl zār, "or spread our hands in prayer toward a foreign god") — pāraś kappayim: to spread/extend the palms in prayer (the upward gesture of supplication). ʾÊl zār: a strange/foreign god.
21: הֲלֹא אֱלֹהִים יַחֲקָר-זֹאת (hălōʾ ʾĕlōhîm yaḥăqār-zōʾt, "Would God not discover this?") — ḥāqar: to search out, to examine thoroughly — the divine forensic examination that reveals everything hidden. כִּי-הוּא יֹדֵעַ תַּעֲלֻמוֹת לֵב (kî-hûʾ yōdêaʿ taʿălumôt lêb, "For he knows the hidden things of the heart") — taʿălumôt: hidden things, secrets (from ʿālam: to conceal). God knows what is concealed even from human self-examination — the argument is: if we had sinned in secret, God would know. The fact of suffering does not imply the fact of hidden sin.
22: כִּי-עָלֶיךָ הֹרַגְנוּ כָל-הַיּוֹם (kî-ʿālêkā hōragnû kol-hayyôm, "Yet for your sake we face death all day long") — the key theological claim: the suffering is "for your sake" (ʿālêkā) — not because of unfaithfulness but because of faithfulness. נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ כְּצֹאן טִבְחָה (neḥshābnû kĕṣōʾn ṭibḥāh, "we are counted as sheep destined for slaughter") — ḥāshab: to be reckoned, to be counted (the accounting root of Ps 32:2) — reckoned as slaughter-sheep. Paul quotes this verse verbatim in Romans 8:36 to describe the condition of believers facing persecution, then immediately follows with: "No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us." The psalm's suffering-for-faithfulness becomes the NT's theology of cross-bearing discipleship.
23: עוּרָה לָמָּה תִישַׁן אֲדֹנָי (ʿûrāh lāmmāh tîshān ʾădōnāy, "Wake up! Why are you sleeping, O Lord?") — ʿûr (qal imperative: to awake, to rouse oneself). The remarkable anthropomorphism: the LORD asleep — not a theological claim that God sleeps (Ps 121:4 explicitly denies this: "He who watches over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep") but the experiential language of a community in extremis. The prayer uses the only vocabulary available to describe the feeling of divine inaction. הָקִיצָה אַל-תִּזְנַח לָנֶצַח (hāqîṣāh ʾal-tiznaḥ lāneṣaḥ, "Rise up! Do not reject us forever!") — qîṣ: to awake; zānaḥ: to reject (as in v9). The fear that the rejection might be permanent (lāneṣaḥ: forever).
24: לָמָּה-פָנֶיךָ תַסְתִּיר (lāmmāh-pānêkā tastîr, "Why do you hide your face?") — hester pānim (hiding of the face — as in Ps 30:7; 31:17): the divine concealment experienced as absence. תִּשְׁכַּח עָנְיֵנוּ וְלַחֲצֵנוּ (tishkaḥ ʿānyênû wĕlaḥăṣênû, "Why do you ignore our affliction and our oppression?") — ʿonyī: affliction, poverty, humiliation (from ʿānāh: to be bent/bowed down); laḥas: oppression (as in Ps 42:9). Both words describe the crushing of a people under superior force.
25: כִּי שָׁחָה לֶעָפָר נַפְשֵׁנוּ (kî shāḥāh leʿāpār napshênû, "For our soul is brought down to the dust") — shāḥāh: to bow, to sink down (the same root as tishtôḥăḥî in Ps 42:5 — the downcast soul); ʿāpār: dust (as in Ps 30:9 — the dust of death). דָּבְקָה לָאָרֶץ בִּטְנֵנוּ (dābĕqāh lāʾāreṣ biṭnênû, "our body clings to the ground") — dābaq: to cling, to stick fast — the intimate bodily contact with the ground in utter prostration.
26: קוּמָה עֶזְרָתָה לָּנוּ (qûmāh ʿezrātāh llānû, "Rise up and help us!") — qûm (imperative: rise!) — the same imperative used for divine waking in v23 but now with the purpose of help (ʿezer). וּפְדֵנוּ לְמַעַן חַסְדֶּךָ (ûpĕdênû lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā, "Deliver us for the sake of your steadfast love") — pādāh: to redeem, to ransom (as in Ps 31:5). Lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā: "for the sake of your covenant-loyalty" — the final appeal grounds the prayer not in the community's merit (which has been affirmed) but in God's own covenant character. The psalm ends not with self-justification but with appeal to divine ḥesed.
